input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system under study is the MABI MAX 100 industrial robot, a six-axis articulated robot with specific focus on the first axis components, including the motor, gearboxes, and Siemens controller. The operational domain is industrial manufacturing, requiring precise motion control. Key characteristics include high precision demands and mechanical wear monitoring needs, making it suitable for digital twinning to enhance maintenance and operational accuracy.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin system utilizes Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) with SysML for multi-level abstraction modeling (requirements, functional, logical, and product/technical layers). Methodologies including RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT manage system complexity. Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs define component interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavior modeling employs MontiArc Automaton and SysML v1.7 state machines, integrated with MathWorks Simulink for simulation, alongside C++ and Java for detailed behavior implementation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Digital Twin systems utilize Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies with SysML for structural modeling (e.g., Internal Block Diagrams for component interfaces and composition) and behavioral modeling (state machines in SysML v1.7). Abstraction levels include requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers, managed by methodologies such as RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT. Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) like ACME, AADL, and MontiArc define component-based system structures. Behavioral and analytical modeling is enhanced using MathWorks Simulink for simulations and general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java) for detailed logic implementation. Data management integrates heterogeneous models and stakeholder perspectives across these abstraction levels.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The physical acting components consist of a permanent magnet synchronous motor driving the first axis, where the motor current (I_M) serves as the input control variable and torque (τ_M) is the output. A gear transmission system, comprising an initial gear stage followed by a cycloidal gear mechanism, is employed to achieve the required low gear ratio. This mechanical configuration enables precise torque transmission and motion control for the actuation system.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing infrastructure includes two position encoders per drive: a motor encoder mounted directly on the motor shaft and a secondary encoder positioned behind the gearbox. This dual-encoder arrangement measures rotational position with high precision, effectively reducing backlash and minimizing positional uncertainty in the system's state feedback.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is maintained through rigorous validation by comparing simulated data against measured physical system data to quantify deviations. Friction models are described with high accuracy to ensure realistic dynamic behavior. The sensor arrangement, including dual position encoders, contributes to reducing positional uncertainty, and quality assurance processes involve continuous error detection and correction mechanisms to ensure data and model validity.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor time series data is transmitted from physical objects to the digital shadow via MQTT protocol in a one-way data flow. The data undergoes aggregation and abstraction as part of contextual data collection for specific purposes.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: MQTT protocol is used for streaming sensor time series data between physical and virtual components. Network topology, bandwidth, latency, reliability, redundancy, and security protocols are not specified in the document.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system complies with industry standards including SysML for systems engineering modeling, ACME and AADL for architecture description languages (ADLs) specifying component & connector structures, and MontiArc for behavior modeling using Automaton and state machines. These standards ensure interoperability among heterogeneous modeling tools and stakeholders as per [31].

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the specific robot (MABI MAX 100), its configuration (six-axis), and the focus on the first axis components (motor, gearboxes, Siemens controller). It correctly identifies the operational domain (manufacturing/machining) and the goal of improving motion accuracy. Quotes: 'The focus of this paper is the serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes', 'The first vertical robot axis at the base is used for creating a digital shadow', 'controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl'. It scores 4 rather than 5 because it summarizes the behavior and monitoring needs rather than providing the in-depth technical behavioral details (e.g., specific encoder arrangement reducing backlash) present in the source.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. However, the source document does mention services provided by the digital shadow, such as 'visualization in a dashboard' and the goal to 'improve the robot’s motion accuracy' (a form of optimization/monitoring). While the document focuses on the *construction* of the digital shadow rather than a comprehensive list of Digital Twin services, claiming it is 'Not in Document' is inaccurate because some services are explicitly described. Therefore, the score is low.

The extraction is highly detailed and grounded in Section II.A 'Modeling Software and Systems'. It accurately lists MBSE, SysML, specific methodologies (RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT), Architecture Description Languages (ACME, AADL, MontiArc, SysML IBDs), and simulation/behavior tools (MontiArc Automaton, SysML v1.7, Simulink, C++, Java).

- "Model-Based Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase."
- "There exist different methodologies on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)... OOSEM... SPES... or SMArDT."

The description precisely matches the physical system description provided in Section III. The extraction provides a detailed and accurate account of the sensing infrastructure described in Section III. The mention of the dual encoder setup reducing uncertainty is also supported. However, the specific phrase 'Quality assurance processes involve continuous error detection and correction mechanisms' is slightly interpretive, as the text focuses on 'continuous identification' and 'traceability' rather than explicit quality assurance processes. Supported by quotes: 'solution scope of the deviation of simulated data versus measured data', 'intended to describe friction as accurately as possible', 'sensor arrangement can reduce backlash and positional uncertainty'.
